230	            Self::Extension(SortCase::ABCabc) => PossibleValue::new("Ext").alias("Extension"),
231	            // “new” sorts oldest at the top and newest at the bottom; “old” sorts newest at the

233	            // “size” puts the smallest at  the top and the largest at the bottom, doesn’t it?

7	//! Parsing command-line strings into exa options.
8	//!
9	//! This module imports exa’s configuration types, such as `View` (the details
10	//! of displaying multiple files) and `DirAction` (what to do when encountering
11	//! a directory), and implements `deduce` methods on them so they can be
12	//! configured using command-line options.
13	//!
14	//!
15	//! ## Useless and overridden options
16	//!
17	//! Let’s say exa was invoked with just one argument: `exa --inode`. The
18	//! `--inode` option is used in the details view, where it adds the inode
19	//! column to the output. But because the details view is *only* activated with
20	//! the `--long` argument, adding `--inode` without it would not have any
21	//! effect.
22	//!
23	//! For a long time, exa’s philosophy was that the user should be warned
24	//! whenever they could be mistaken like this. If you tell exa to display the
25	//! inode, and it *doesn’t* display the inode, isn’t that more annoying than
26	//! having it throw an error back at you?
27	//!
28	//! However, this doesn’t take into account *configuration*. Say a user wants
29	//! to configure exa so that it lists inodes in the details view, but otherwise
30	//! functions normally. A common way to do this for command-line programs is to
31	//! define a shell alias that specifies the details they want to use every
32	//! time. For the inode column, the alias would be:
33	//!
34	//! `alias exa="exa --inode"`
35	//!
36	//! Using this alias means that although the inode column will be shown in the
37	//! details view, you’re now *only* allowed to use the details view, as any
38	//! other view type will result in an error. Oops!
39	//!
40	//! Another example is when an option is specified twice, such as `exa
41	//! --sort=Name --sort=size`. Did the user change their mind about sorting, and
42	//! accidentally specify the option twice?
43	//!
44	//! Again, exa rejected this case, throwing an error back to the user instead
45	//! of trying to guess how they want their output sorted. And again, this
46	//! doesn’t take into account aliases being used to set defaults. A user who
47	//! wants their files to be sorted case-insensitively may configure their shell
48	//! with the following:
49	//!
50	//! `alias exa="exa --sort=Name"`
51	//!
52	//! Just like the earlier example, the user now can’t use any other sort order,
53	//! because exa refuses to guess which one they meant. It’s *more* annoying to
54	//! have to go back and edit the command than if there were no error.
55	//!
56	//! Fortunately, there’s a heuristic for telling which options came from an
57	//! alias and which came from the actual command-line: aliased options are
58	//! nearer the beginning of the options array, and command-line options are
59	//! nearer the end. This means that after the options have been parsed, exa
60	//! needs to traverse them *backwards* to find the last-most-specified one.
61	//!
62	//! For example, invoking exa with `exa --sort=size` when that alias is present
63	//! would result in a full command-line of:
64	//!
65	//! `exa --sort=Name --sort=size`
66	//!
67	//! `--sort=size` should override `--sort=Name` because it’s closer to the end
68	//! of the arguments array. In fact, because there’s no way to tell where the
69	//! arguments came from — it’s just a heuristic — this will still work even
70	//! if no aliases are being used!
71	//!
72	//! Finally, this isn’t just useful when options could override each other.
73	//! Creating an alias `exal="exa --long --inode --header"` then invoking `exal
74	//! --grid --long` shouldn’t complain about `--long` being given twice when
75	//! it’s clear what the user wants.

105	    /// The action to perform when encountering a directory rather than a
106	    /// regular file.

113	    /// grid-details) along with the options on how to render file names.
114	    /// If the view requires the terminal to have a width, and there is no
115	    /// width, then the view will be downgraded.
116	    pub view: View,
117	
118	    /// The options to make up the styles of the UI and file names.
119	    pub theme: ThemeOptions,

121	    /// Whether to read file names from stdin instead of the command-line
122	    pub stdin: FilesInput,

126	    /// Whether the View specified in this set of options includes a Git
127	    /// status column. It’s only worth trying to discover a repository if the
128	    /// results will end up being displayed.
129	    #[must_use]
130	    pub fn should_scan_for_git(&self) -> bool {
131	        if self.filter.git_ignore == GitIgnore::CheckAndIgnore {
132	            return true;
133	        }
134	
135	        match self.view.mode {
136	            Mode::Details(details::Options {
137	                table: Some(ref table),
138	                ..
139	            })
140	            | Mode::GridDetails(grid_details::Options {
141	                details:
142	                    details::Options {
143	                        table: Some(ref table),
144	                        ..
145	                    },
146	                ..
147	            }) => table.columns.git,
148	            _ => false,
149	        }
150	    }
151	
152	    /// Determines the complete set of options based on the given command-line
153	    /// arguments, after they’ve been parsed.
154	    pub fn deduce<V: Vars>(matches: &ArgMatches, vars: &V) -> Result<Self, OptionsError> {
155	        if cfg!(not(feature = "git")) && (matches.get_flag("git") || matches.get_flag("git-ignore"))
156	        {
157	            return Err(OptionsError::Unsupported(String::from(
158	                "Options --git and --git-ignore can't be used because `git` feature was disabled in this build of exa",
159	            )));
160	        }
161	        let strict = vars
162	            .get_with_fallback(vars::EXA_STRICT, vars::EZA_STRICT)
163	            .is_some();
164	
165	        let view = View::deduce(matches, vars, strict)?;
166	        let dir_action = DirAction::deduce(matches, matches!(view.mode, Mode::Details(_)), strict)?;
167	        let filter = FileFilter::deduce(matches, strict)?;
168	        let theme = ThemeOptions::deduce(matches, vars);
169	        let stdin = FilesInput::deduce(matches, vars);
170	
171	        Ok(Self {
172	            dir_action,
173	            filter,
174	            view,
175	            theme,
176	            stdin,
177	        })
178	    }

content.text:
Here's a summary of the changes made to fix the bug:

## Summary

The bug was that `--hyperlink` always added hyperlink escape sequences to output, even when piped. The feature request was to make `--hyperlink` work like `--color` and `--icons` with `always/auto/never` modes.

### Changes Made (3 files):

1. **`src/output/file_name.rs`** — Updated the `EmbedHyperlinks` enum:
   - Changed from `Off/On` to `Always/Automatic/Never` (with `Never` as default)
   - Updated `escaped_file_name()` to check `is_a_tty` when in `Automatic` mode (suppresses hyperlinks when output is piped)
   - Updated the link target display code that used `EmbedHyperlinks::Off` → `EmbedHyperlinks::Never`

2. **`src/options/file_name.rs`** — Updated `EmbedHyperlinks::deduce()`:
   - Now reads `ShowWhen` values from the `--hyperlink` argument instead of just checking a boolean flag
   - Added 5 new test cases to cover the new behavior (auto/default, always, never explicit, auto explicit)

3. **`src/options/parser.rs`** — Updated the `--hyperlink` CLI argument definition:
   - Changed from a simple flag to a `ShowWhen`-based argument with `num_args(0..=1)` and `default_missing_value("auto")`, matching the pattern used by `--icons` and `--classify`

### Behavior:
- **`--hyperlink`** (no value or `auto`): Hyperlinks only when output is a TTY, suppressed when piped
- **`--hyperlink always`**: Always embed hyperlinks, even when piped
- **`--hyperlink never`**: Never embed hyperlinks

Here's a summary of the changes made to fix the bug:

## Summary

The bug was that `--hyperlink` always added hyperlink escape sequences to output, even when piped. The feature request was to make `--hyperlink` work like `--color` and `--icons` with `always/auto/never` modes.

### Changes Made (3 files):

1. **`src/output/file_name.rs`** — Updated the `EmbedHyperlinks` enum:
   - Changed from `Off/On` to `Always/Automatic/Never` (with `Never` as default)
   - Updated `escaped_file_name()` to check `is_a_tty` when in `Automatic` mode (suppresses hyperlinks when output is piped)
   - Updated the link target display code that used `EmbedHyperlinks::Off` → `EmbedHyperlinks::Never`

2. **`src/options/file_name.rs`** — Updated `EmbedHyperlinks::deduce()`:
   - Now reads `ShowWhen` values from the `--hyperlink` argument instead of just checking a boolean flag
   - Added 5 new test cases to cover the new behavior (auto/default, always, never explicit, auto explicit)

3. **`src/options/parser.rs`** — Updated the `--hyperlink` CLI argument definition:
   - Changed from a simple flag to a `ShowWhen`-based argument with `num_args(0..=1)` and `default_missing_value("auto")`, matching the pattern used by `--icons` and `--classify`

### Behavior:
- **`--hyperlink`** (no value or `auto`): Hyperlinks only when output is a TTY, suppressed when piped
- **`--hyperlink always`**: Always embed hyperlinks, even when piped
- **`--hyperlink never`**: Never embed hyperlinks
